{{nihongo|'''Atsuta Jingū'''|熱田神宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Owari Province]].

'''Atsuta Shrine'' (Atsuta Jingu) is {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|qid=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|qid=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}{{ill|lt=Atsuta Ward|Q1355625|qid=Q1355625|1=Atsuta-ku, Nagoya}}{{jalink|Jingu {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in (Nagoya City)|Q30934394|Jingu}}. {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|qid=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. It is a shrine deeply related to {{ill|lt=Japanese mythology|Q814158|qid=Q814158|1=Japanese mythology}} that enshrines the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kusanagi sword|1=UNKNOWN}}, and has collected {{ill|lt=faith|Q5410500|qid=Q5410500|1=Faith}} from {{ill|lt=ancient times|Q41493|qid=Q41493|1=Ancient history}} as "Atsuta shrine."

=== Overview ===
[[File:Minami Torii in Atsuta Jingu Shrine 2014.jpg|thumb|Minami Torii and New Year's visit crowd<br />(January 2014)]]
It is located at the southern end of {{ill|lt=Atsuta Plateau|Q30922940|qid=Q30922940|1=Atsuta Plateau}} in the southern part of {{ill|lt=Nagoya City|Q11751|qid=Q11751|1=Nagoya}}. In ancient times, it was located on a protruding {{ill|lt=cape|Q185113|qid=Q185113|1=Cape (geography)}} in {{ill|lt=Ise Bay|Q1368308|qid=Q1368308|1=Ise Bay}}, but now that the surrounding area has been {{ill|lt=reclamation|Q106259|qid=Q106259|1=Polder}}, no trace of it can be seen. </ref>.It is known as a shrine that enshrines the {{ill|lt=Amamuragumo Sword|Q14944|qid=Q14944|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}}, one of the {{ill|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|Q327465|qid=Q327465|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}. Even after it was enshrined, this sword was stolen (see "{{ill|lt=Kusanagi sword theft incident|Q861812|qid=Q861812|1=theft of Kusanagi no Tsurugi}}"), {{ill|lt=Katayo|Q11487867|qid=Q11487867|1=Katashiro}} He has suffered many hardships, including being lost during the {{ill|lt=Battle of Dannoura|Q968676|qid=Q968676|1=Battle of Dan-no-ura}} (see "{{ill|lt=Amamourakumo Ken|Q14944|qid=Q14944|1=Kusanagi no Tsurugi}}"). Although there are various theories, the Kusanagi Shinken was founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} in 1943. Atsuta Shrine] (Atsuta Shrine official website, accessed on November 7, 2018)</ref>, Atsuta Shrine was founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} {{#tag:ref|``The construction of the shrine was around the first year of the same Emperor (Emperor Chuai).'' (``Atsuta Jingu Abbreviation'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials 344">Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: 344 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}} or 646 ({{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|qid=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}}2) ) {{#tag:ref|``The God (Atsuta Daimyojin) received <sub>㆑</sub>入-<sub>㆓</sub>Zaōjo<small>ni</small><sub>㆒</sub><ruby><rb>Apology Sen</rb><rt>Kotakusen</rt></ruby><small>ni</small><ruby><rb>偁</rb><rt>Iha</rt></ruby><small>ku</small >, uby><rb>Esaki</rb><rt>Esaki</rt></ruby><ruby><rb>Matsukojima</rb><rt>Matsukonoshima</rt></ruby><ruby><rb>Kiya</rb>< rt>Hatayano</rt></ruby>village<small>ni</small>to<small>te</small><sub>㆓</sub>to<small>ru</small><ruby><rb> Dahua >ヲ</small><sub>㆒</sub>, written by Tenka<small>ri</small><ruby><rb>za</rb><rt>Mashimasu</rt></ruby>Kinya,'' (``Shucho Kanfu'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials 13">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: Engihito History Edition'': page 13</ref>)|group="Note"}} In ancient times, it had a strong presence as a regional shrine in {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|qid=Q514506|1=Owari Province}} (currently the western region of Aichi Prefecture), and after the Middle Ages it rapidly rose politically and economically, becoming the ``Japan's Third National Shrine.'' name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials Auspicious History 21">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Auspicious History Edition": page 21</ref>" ({{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|qid=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ja link|Iwashimizu Hachimangu|Q710098}}) (``Atsuta {{ill|lt=Hatsumode|Q1032381|qid=Q1032381|1=Hatsumōde}} Over 2 million worshipers visit every year <ref>[https://www.mapple.net/xp/hatsumode 6th place Atsuta Jingu [Aichi Prefecture] approximately 2.3 million people]</ref>.

=== Enshrined God ===
; Main enshrined deity
* '''Atsuta Okami'' (Atsuta no Okami)
** One of the {{ill|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|Q327465|qid=Q327465|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} '''Kusanagi Shinken'' (Kusanagi Mitsurugi, also Kusanagi Sword and Amamuragumo Sword) <ref>``Kusanagi Divine Sword'': Official name at Atsuta Shrine. <ref>Shrine history book which says that </ref> refers to '''{{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Amaterasu|qid=Amaterasu|ltq=Q455602}}''', which is {{ill|lt=divine body|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}}. </ref>.

; Aidenjin
* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} (Amaterasu wolf)
* {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
* {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} (Yamato Takeru Mikoto)
* {{ill|lt=Miyasuhime|Q11455216|qid=Q11455216|lt=Miyasuhime no Mikoto|1=Miyazu-hime}}
* {{ill|lt=Takeinadane no Mikoto|Q11486691|qid=Q11486691|1=Takeinadane}}Regarding Atsuta Omikami, the main deity enshrined, Atsuta Shrine says, ``Amaterasu Omikami, who brings forth the Kusanagi Sword, one of the three sacred treasures, as a divine spirit.''<ref name="Jingu 7">Atsuta Jingu: Page 7</ref> In other words, it refers to Amaterasu Omikami as the ``true identity'' of the Kusanagi Sword; in other words, the Kusanagi Sword itself is ``Atsuta Omikami'' as the ``substance'' of Amaterasu Omikami. {{ill|lt=Before the war#"Before the war" in Japanese history|Q7239276|qid=Q7239276|lt=Before the war|1=Pre-war}}, the main enshrined deity was "Kusanagi Omikken-jin" ("Atsuta Jingu abbreviation" (1877 (Meiji 10))) <ref name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials Auspicious Origin History 339">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Auspicious Origin Edition": page 339</ref>, "Amatsumi Shirushi Chrysalis Ohomitsurugi" ("Atsuta Jingu Abbreviation" (1939)) <ref name="abbreviation 1">``Atsuta Jingu abbreviation'': 1 page</ref>, but no distinction was made between the true identity and the spirit age in terms of expression. However, the idea that the Kusanagi sword is Amaterasu Omikami is an old one that already appears in the Atsuta Myojin Koshiki (late Heian period). /rt></ruby>'''Amaterasu Omikami's true form'', Konno Omiya Gongen Koreya (If you listen to the classic scriptures, Murakumo is the true identity of Amaterasu Omiya, the present Omiya Gongen.)'' (Atsuta Myojin Lectures <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials 22">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: 22 pages")|group="Note"}}. However, in the older ``{{ill|lt=Owari Province Fudoki|Q135598023|qid=Q135598023|1=Owari no Kuni Fudoki}},'' there is an image of Japan Takeru when he handed over the Kusanagi sword to Miyazaki Hime. {{#tag:ref | Indeed, this sword has the <ruby><rb>spirit</rb><rt> of God</rt></ruby>. Mikage</rt></ruby> and <ruby><rb><br></rb><rt>Set</rt></ruby>, and <ruby><rb>Cause</rb><rt></rt></ruby> set up <ruby><rb>company</rb><rt>Yashiro</rt></ruby>, <ruby><r The name is derived from <ruby><rb>y</rb><rt> from b>鄕</rb><rt>sato</rt></ruby> (Atsuta-go).'' name="Fudōki 246">``Complete Japanese Classical Works ``Fudōki'' Part 2'': page 246</ref>)|group="Note"}}, it is said that in the Nara period there was a view that Japan Takeru was the true identity of the Kusanagi Shinken.<ref name="Shin-Nagoya City History 1_524">"New Nagoya City History Volume 1": 524 pages</ref> Since the Heian period, it has been a common belief that the true identity of the Kusanagi sword is Amaterasu Omikami, the deity enshrined at the Imperial Shrine (Inner Shrine) in Ise. name="Shin-Nagoya City History 2_334">"New Nagoya City History Volume 2": page 334 It has even been said that they are the two supporting pillars. ``Nihon Ni''''<ruby><rb>'''并''''</rb><rt>Hashira</rt></ruby>'''The God'' The name of Japan's divine kingdom is for the benefit of sentient beings, the place of the Southern Enfudai, the name of Shinmei is given, and the birth of the Buddha is the most important thing. name="Jingu Historical Materials 28">Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: 28 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}}.The shrine enshrines Amaterasu Omikami, Sujin no Mikoto, Nihon Takeru, Miyasa Hime no Mikoto, Kenine Tane no Mikoto, and other gods related to Kusanagi Tsurugi. When Sujinson defeated {{ill|lt=Yamata no Orochi|Q1054437|qid=Q1054437|1=Yamata no Orochi}}, he found the Kusanagi sword in the tail of Yamata no Orochi and presented it to Amaterasu Omikami. Amaterasu Omikami gave the Kusanagi sword to {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|qid=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} (Ninigi no Mikoto) at the time of {{ill|lt=Descent|Q1084733|qid=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}. After taking the Kusanagi sword and playing an active role in {{ill|lt=Ezo conquest|UNKNOWN|lt=Ezo conquest}}, Takeru of Japan entrusted it to his wife, Miyazaki Hime-no-Mikoto. Miyazakihime no Mikoto established the land of Atsuta as {{ill|lt=Fortune telling#Boku|Q1043197|qid=Q1043197|lt=Bokusei|1=Divination}} and enshrined the Kusanagi sword. Kenine Tane-no-Mikoto was the older brother of Miyasa Hime-no-Mikoto, and served as an adjutant general in Japan Takeru's conquest of Ezo.As the ``Engishiki'' Volume 3 God Gizo (temporary festival) and Volume 9 Shinto Name Book say ``Atsuta Shrine Ichiza'' <ref name="Engishiki 194">"Engishiki 1": page 94</ref><ref name="Engishiki 2123">"Engishiki 2nd": page 123</ref>, it is highly likely that only the sacred sword was enshrined until ancient times.<ref name="Shin-Nagoya City History 1_522">"New Nagoya City History Volume 1": 522 pages</ref>. According to legend, the sacred sword was transferred from the capital in 686 (the first year of Shucho), but in reality, probably after the Middle Ages when the influence of the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism became evident, the sacred sword was enshrined in the Doyoden and the five gods were enshrined in the Seiden.{{# tag:ref|``At the time of Emperor Tenmu's return from Miyako (the capital), he rebuilt the Doyo Palace, put the sacred sword in a stone chest, and enshrined it in Dochu.'' (Bishu Jingu Hiden) <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials, Origin and History Sequel 3">'Atsuta Shrine Historical Materials, Engiance and History Sequel (1)': 3 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}}. After Doyoden was abolished in 1893 (Meiji 26), the main shrine was enshrined with the five deities of the main deity, the Kusanagi sword and the Aiden deity. name="Shin-Nagoya City History 1_522-523">"New Nagoya City History Volume 1": pages 522-523</ref>.

=== Founding and founding ===
The Kusanagi divine sword is said to have been born from the tail of Sosanagi when he defeated {{ill|lt=Yamata no Orochi|Q1054437|qid=Q1054437|1=Yamata no Orochi}}. b>Cut the <ruby><rb>snake</rb><rt>orochi</rt></ruby> on the <ruby><rb>tail</rb><rt>o</rt></ruby>. As it grows, the <ruby><rb>sword</rb><rt> of the <ruby><rb>sword</rt></ruby> will not come</rt></ruby>, so if you pry its tail open, there will be a sword inside. ``It's Yuru<ruby><rb>Kusanagi</rb><rt>Kusanagi no</rt></ruby><ruby><rb>Tsurugi</rt></ruby>'' (Nihon Shoki (Vol. 1 Jindai 8th section) <ref. name="Nihon Shoki 1 67">"Kana Nihon Shoki Volume 1": page 67</ref>)|group="Note"}}. It appears in "Kojiki" (Volume 1) as "Tsumugari no Tachi" (a beautiful name for a sharp long sword).<ref name="Kojiki 29">"Front Notes Konbun Kojiki": page 29</ref>, "Nihonshoki" (Volume 1, Jindai, 8th section, 1st book) states that the original name is "'''Ama no Murakumo no Tsurugi'"<ref name="Nihonshoki 1 67">"Kana Nihonshoki Volume 1": page 67</ref>. According to one theory, when Amaterasu Omikami was invited from {{ill|lt=Amaniwato|Q3231835|qid=Q3231835|lt=Amaniwaya|1=Amano-Iwato}}, it was worshiped along with the Yata mirror as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=reishi|1=UNKNOWN}} (Iyajiro). It is said that when he sat in the Ten Iwaya, he was offered the three sacred treasures of the sacred mirror, the sacred sword, and the divine jewel, as well as the ritual fee for the prayers he invited and offered..." (Atsuta Jingu Abbreviation <ref name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials 340">Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: 340 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}}, it was later stolen by Yamata no Orochi, but it is said that Sujinson retrieved it.{{#tag:ref |``The original <ruby><rb>Amaterasu Omikami</rb><rt>Amaterasu Ohomikami</rt></ruby> lives <ruby><rb></rb><rt> in Izumo Province</rt></ruby><ruby><rb> The Yamata serpent</rb><rt>Yamata wo Rochi</rt></ruby><ruby><rb>Evil god</rb><rt>Magami</rt></ruby> steals and steals it, because the <small>Yamata serpent lives there and stands like a cloud. The sword of Murakumo is said to be the younger brother of Oomikami<ruby><rb>Kenhaya Susanooson</rb><rt>Take possesses the power of ease</rt></ruby> and is offered to Oomikami. name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials 335">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials: 335 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}}, as this sacred sword was presented or returned to Amaterasu Omikami by Sujinson, it became a solemn "spiritual object of Omikami" {{#tag:ref|"Atsuta Jingu Abbreviation"<ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Auspicious History 340"/>|group="Note"}} It will wield divine authority.When Amaterasu Omikami handed down the {{ill|lt=divine edict|Q11588781|qid=Q11588781|1=Commandment}} of the descending of her Tenson, she put her soul into this divine sword, and along with the divine mirror (Yatakagami) and the divine seal (Hasshaku Qiong Magatama), she created {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Ninigi no Mikoto). Since then, the imperial family has enshrined it in the Imperial Palace as the guardian of the Treasure House (three types and nine sacred treasures). by>, <ruby><rb>{{ill|lt=Japanese Great Spirit God|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese Great Spirit}}</rb><rt>Yamato no Ohokunitama</rt></ruby>, the two-hashira god, <ruby><rb>Emperor</rb> In the <rt>Suheramikoto</rt></ruby> <ruby><rb>Great Hall</rb><rt>Miaraka</rt></ruby>, there is a Himatsuri.'' (Nihon Shoki (Volume 5, Emperor Sujin) <ref name="Nihon Shoki 1 205">"Kana Nihon Shoki Volume 1": 205 pages</ref>)|group="Note"}}. There was a certain maple tree that set itself on fire and continued to burn, and even when it fell into the rice field, the flame did not go out and the rice field was still hot. It is said that this place was named Atsuta and was designated as a shrine site. . . . Mizuta Naotsu. Atsutasha <sup>㆒</sup>. name="Group document 1_858"/>)|group="Note"}}. Then, a shrine was built in Hikami-mura, where Hime was located, and the divine spirit was enshrined as Hikami Aneko Tenjin. p>Sakura-<sup>㆓</sup>Festival <sup>㆒</sup>. name="Gunshoju 1_859">Gunshoju 1st collection: page 859</ref>)|group="Note"}}.The story of the establishment of Atsuta Shrine and Hikami Aneko Shrine according to the ``Owari Province Atsuta Daijingu Enki'' is as described above. This book is said to mainly quote ``Nihonshoki'' from many sources including ``Kojiki'' and ``Owari Province Fudoki,'' and the book itself is said to have little originality. On the other hand, it is said that there are few distortions of the original text, and faithful quotations are made, and even though it was written in the Kamakura period, the content itself is said to have documentary value as being from the Heian period or earlier. Above all, this book is unique in that it describes how Kenine Tane-no-Mikoto guided him to the mansion in Hikami-mura, where he met Miyazuhime and spent their days together, and how he stopped by Hikami-mura again on his way back from his eastern expedition. The statement that Atsuta was chosen as the place of worship by fortune-telling indicates that the sacred treasure of Amaterasu Omikami was brought to Atsuta via the imperial family's Ise Shrine, and is an important point for Atsuta Shrine to flaunt its noble legitimacy.However, as {{ill|lt=Hisaya Ozaki|Q11465166|qid=Q11465166|1=Kyūya Osaki}} points out, "Owari Province Atsuta Daijingu Enki" explains why the place chosen for the shrine (Atsuta) came to be called Atsuta. Although the location of the shrine was determined, there is no record of when the shrine was founded and the Kusanagi Shinken was transferred there, or whether the shrine itself was founded or relocated.<ref name="Historical Materials Review 36">"Atsuta Shrine Historical Materials Review": 36 pages</ref>. Furthermore, the place where Hime protected and worshiped the Kusanagi sword after her death, that is, the place where it was transferred, is actually vaguely described (it does not say that it was Atsuta or Hikami-mura) <ref name="Historical Reference 36"/>. The ambiguity of ``Owari Province Atsuta Daijingu Enki'' was supplemented by various enki and history books that appeared in later years.

=== History ===
{{Attribution of source|date=Monday, August 7, 2017 07:49 (UTC)|section=1}}

=== Outline ===
The {{ill|lt=guji|Q11453500|qid=Q11453500|lt=grand chief priest|1=Gūji}} position was held by the {{ill|lt=Owari clan|Q11465311|qid=Q11465311|1=Owari clan}}, a descendant of Kunizo Owari, for generations, but in the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}} The position was given to {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Tokinori|Q11623142|qid=Q11623142|1=Fujiwara no Suenori}} of {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Minami family|Q2975332|qid=Q2975332|1=Fujiwara Nan-ke}}, who was {{ill|lt=grandson|Q11430412|qid=Q11430412|1=grandson}} of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Owara member position|1=UNKNOWN}}. Since then, the descendants of the {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Minami family|Q2975332|qid=Q2975332|1=Fujiwara Nan-ke}}{{ill|lt=Fujiwara clan|Q641628|qid=Q641628|1=Fujiwara clan}} and {{Ruby|[[:Category:Chiaki family|Chiaki]]|senshu}} families have continued to serve as Daiguji, and the Owari clan has continued to serve as Gonguji. Furthermore, this daughter of Suenori is the mother of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|qid=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} ({{ill|lt=Yura Gozen|Q11577620|qid=Q11577620|1=Yura Gozen}}). In addition, Suenori's adopted daughter (real grandchild) was married to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshiyasu Ashikaga|1=UNKNOWN}} (ancestor of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga clan|Q725370|qid=Q725370|1=Ashikaga clan}}), and this lineage has been passed down to the Ashikaga clan as well. Furthermore, Kihan's younger sister is the mother of {{ill|lt=Nikaido Administration|Q11371903|qid=Q11371903|1=Nikaidō Yukimasa}}, and has a close relationship with {{ill|lt=Mr. Nikaido|Q2000952|qid=Q2000952|1=Nikaidō clan}}.

``{{ill|lt=Kaidoki|Q11559314|qid=Q11559314|1=Kaidōki}}'' says, ``If you pass the palace of Atsuta no Miya,'' and ``{{ill|lt=Tokan Kiko|Q11528660|qid=Q11528660|lt=Tokan Kiko|1=Tōkan Kikō}}'' says, ``You will arrive at Atsuta no Miya, the land of Owari.'' Also in the same book,
{{Quotation|Someone said, ``This shrine is Susano no Mikoto, and the shrine was first built in the country of Izumo.Yakumo stands [(*') Yakumo Tatsu Izumo Yaegaki Build a Yaegaki in Tsumago (*') (Kojiki)] This is also the origin of the Yamato word. Later, during the reign of Emperor Keiko, this stone was left behind. ” he said. He also says, ``The main body of this shrine is the sacred sword that is dedicated to Kusanagi.When Keiyuki's son, Nihon Takeru, returns home after giving up his life, he becomes a swan and departs, and his sword rests in Atsuta.''
|Kokumin Tosho Co., Ltd.|Tokyo Travelogue (School Note: Nippon Bungaku University 3)}}
It is stated that.

{{ill|lt=Zuikei Shuho|Q11573918|qid=Q11573918|1=Zuikei Shūhō}}'s Gaun Diary {{ill|lt=1465|Q6556|qid=Q6556|1=1465}} ({{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|qid=Q1143928|1=Kanshō}}6) June 18th article states, ``'''Japan's so-called three great chief priests'''' , Itsukushima, Atsuta, and Fujino Sanshoya,'' and Atsuta Daiguji is counted as one of Japan's three great chief priests, along with {{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shinto family|Q11410131|qid=Q11410131|1=Itsukushima kannushi family}} and {{ill|lt=Fuji clan|Q11456091|qid=Q11456091|1=Fuji clan}}<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese book|author={{ill|lt=University of Tokyo Historiographical Institute|Q11524667|qid=Q11524667|1=Historiographical Institute}}|title=Dainippon Ancient Records Gaun Nichirenroku Extract|date=1961|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=|pages=161}}</ref>.{{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|qid=Q204023|lt=In the Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the Chiaki family served the Oda family as military commanders, and {{ill|lt=Kimitsu Chiaki|Q95512679|qid=Q95512679|1=Kimitsu Chiaki}} was {{ill|lt=Nobuhide Oda In|Q1153962|qid=Q1153962|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, Suimitsu's child {{ill|lt=Chiaki Suitada|Q11405793|qid=Q11405793|lt=Suitada|1=Senshū Suetada}} serves {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. Before the {{ill|lt=Battle of Okehazama|Q349422|qid=Q349422|1=Battle of Okehazama}}, Nobunaga prayed for victory at Atsuta Shrine and won a spectacular victory, but Suetada died in the battle, and Nobunaga inherited the post of Grand Shrine and his estate to Suetada's son {{ill|lt=Chiaki Suenobu|Q116937389|qid=Q116937389|lt=Suenobu|1=Senshū Suenobu}}, who was still in the womb.

{{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} is located near our company. 33374}} was established, and {{ill|lt=Shichiri no Ferry|Q11353711|qid=Q11353711|1=Shichiri Ferry}} was in operation from this area to {{ill|lt=Kuwana-juku|Q3069785|qid=Q3069785|1=Kuwana-juku}}. Also, it is described as ``Atsuta Daijingu'' in ``{{ill|lt=Tokaido Famous Places Encyclopedia|Q48746095|qid=Q48746095|1=Tōkaidō meisho zue}}''.In June of {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|qid=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 4th year ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}}), it was declared {{ill|lt=Jingu|Q120680118|qid=Q120680118|1=List of Jingū}} and the name was changed from Atsuta Shrine to Atsuta Jingu. {{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}{{ill|lt=July 1st|Q2700|qid=Q2700|1=July 1}} (Meiji 4th year {{jalink|May 14th With the establishment of the {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} on (old calendar)|Q839544|May 14th}}, Atsuta Shrine was ranked as {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System#Kansha|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. There was an argument that Atsuta Jingu ``enshrines one of the three sacred treasures, so it should be on the same level as {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|qid=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}'', and in July of the same year, Grand Priest Chiaki Kifuku petitioned the government to give it the same treatment as Ise Jingu, but this petition was rejected. Tadayuki Tsunoda, who subsequently became Daiguji, continued to make similar petitions, and by {{ill|lt=1889|Q7831|qid=Q7831|1=1889}} (Meiji 22), the divine seal and the establishment of a Gonguji, similar to those at Ise Grand Shrine, were approved.Until then, Atsuta Shrine had been built in Owari-zukuri, an architectural style unique to the Owari region, but in 1889 (Meiji 22), plans were made to construct the shrine building in the same Shinmei-zukuri style as Ise Jingu. Furthermore, as a result of Atsuta Jingu's approach to the government, in September 1890|Q7832}, a proposal was submitted to the Cabinet to remove it from the status of a shrine and make it the same status as Ise Jingu (at the draft stage, the proposal included renaming Atsuta Jingu to ``Owari Jingu'', but this was removed). However, this imperial edict was rejected, and the status of Atsuta Jingu as a shrine remained as before. It is said that the reason behind this was opposition from Ise Grand Shrine. Construction of the shrine building in Shinmei-zukuri style continued and was completed in {{ill|lt=1893|Q7840|qid=Q7840|1=1893}} (Meiji 26). However, this shrine building, along with other buildings, was attacked by air raids during {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|qid=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}.

First of all, in the air raid on March 12th, {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|qid=Q5240|1=1945}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}20), the Chinkomon Gate, Togakusho, Kagura Hall, Shinto Talisman Awarding Office, Imperial Embassy, Saikan, Imperial Palace, etc. were destroyed by fire. |publisher=Tokyo Shoseki |year=2016-09-29 |page=610 |isbn=978-4-487-74409-1}}</ref>. It was then burnt down in the {{ill|lt=Atsuta air raid|Q11569122|qid=Q11569122|1=Bombing of Atsuta}} on June 9 of the same year.Also, just before the end of the war, a temporary relocation to {{ill|lt=Hida Ichinomiya Mizunashi Shrine|Q704865|qid=Q704865|1=Minashi Shrine}} was planned to protect the sacred Kusanagi sword, but this was temporarily canceled due to the end of the war on {{ill|lt=August 15th|Q2774|qid=Q2774|1=August 15}} of the same year. However, because there was a risk that the deity would be stolen by {{ill|lt=American troops|Q11211|qid=Q11211|1=United States Armed Forces}} who had landed this time, on {{ill|lt=August 21|Q2823|qid=Q2823|1=August 21}} of the same year, with the cooperation of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Japanese Army|Q276535|qid=Q276535|lt=Army|1=Imperial Japanese Army}}, the deity was transferred to Mizunashi Shrine as planned. It was returned to Atsuta Shrine in the same year {{ill|lt=September 19|Q2896|qid=Q2896|1=September 19}}, but by then the army had already been disbanded, so the priests were moved by railway.

In {{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|qid=Q5165|1=1948}} (Showa 23), it was added to {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The shrine building was rebuilt in October 1955|Q18577}} (Showa 30), taking over old materials from Ise Grand Shrine's {{ill|lt=Jingu Shikinen Sengu|Q3959472|qid=Q3959472|lt=Shikinen Sengu|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū}}. Because it is a new building, it is not a designated cultural property.

=== Chronology ===
〈〉 indicates related matters.
*(Tradition) Year of Emperor Koan - Hiwarimiko Shrine is founded <ref name="Jingu 20">'Atsuta Jingu': page 20</ref>.
*(Tradition) Before the reign of Emperor Keiko - Magowakamiko Shrine is built (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Auspicious History 320">``Atsuta Shrine Historical Materials Auspicious History Edition'': page 320</ref>).
*(Tradition) Emperor Keiko's 43rd year - {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} passed away in Nobono. The Kusanagi sword is enshrined.
*(Tradition) During the reign of Emperor Keiko - Ichinogozen Shrine is established (``Atsuta Daijingu Enshrined Deity Shintai Honki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engiyoshi 139">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engiyoshi 139'': page 139</ref>).
*(Tradition) 4th year of Emperor Chuai - Miyakami Hime-no-Mikoto dies, and a shrine (Hikami Aneko Shrine) is built on the grounds of Motomiya ("Owari Province Hikami Shrine Start Seiden Honki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engi Yoshiro 405" />).
*{{ill|lt=668|Q30116|qid=Q30116|1=668}} ({{ill|lt=Emperor Tenchi|Q314802|qid=Q314802|1=Emperor Tenji}}7 years) - The Kusanagi sword was stolen by a monk Dogyo of {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|1=Silla}} ("Nihon Shoki") ({{ill|lt=Kusanagi sword theft case|Q861812|qid=Q861812|1=theft of Kusanagi no Tsurugi}}).
*Emperor Tenmu year - Ota Shrine is founded (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engihiro 320"/>).**Same year - Shimochigamama Shrine is built (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engihito 329">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engihito Hen'': page 329</ref>).
**Same year - Kamichi Gama Shrine is built (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engihiro 325">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engihiro Hen'': page 325</ref>).
**Same year - Ryu Shrine is built (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials Engihiro 318">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engihiro Hen'': page 318</ref>).
**Same year - Seizu Shrine is built (``Atsuta Daijingu Sonmyo Matsuri Shinki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engihito 316">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engihito Hen'': page 316</ref>).
*{{ill|lt=686|Q30619|qid=Q30619|1=686}} ({{ill|lt=Shucho|Q777413|qid=Q777413|1=Shuchō}} first year) - The Kusanagi sword is returned to Atsuta Shrine ("Nihon Shoki").
*686 (1st year of Shucho) - Owari Inaokimi takes the title of Daiguji (``Atsuta Daiguji Chiaki family history'' <ref name="Chiaki family documents 2nd volume 312">``Atsuta Jingu documents, Chiaki family documents, volume 2'': page 312</ref>).
*{{ill|lt=708 years|Q23511|qid=Q23511|1=708}} ({{ill|lt=Wado|Q1373026|qid=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} 1st year) September 9 - Hakkengu Shrine is founded (``Atsuta Jingu Shintai Biography'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engi History 157">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engi History Edition'': page 157</ref>).**There is also a theory that it was founded in 686 (the first year of Shucho) (``Atsuta Jingu Seienki'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engi History 131">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engi History Edition'': page 131</ref>).
*{{ill|lt=712 years|Q23521|qid=Q23521|1=712}} (Wado 5th year) January 28 - <Completion of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}"
*{{ill|lt=720 years|Q23539|qid=Q23539|1=720}} ({{ill|lt=Yoro|Q1373061|qid=Q1373061|1=Yōrō}}4 years) - <Completion of "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}"
*Mid-9th century - Jingu-ji Temple is built<ref name="Shin-Nagoya City History 2_335">"New Nagoya City History Volume 2": page 335</ref>.
*{{ill|lt=907 years|Q24163|qid=Q24163|1=907}} ({{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|qid=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}}7 years) - Added to {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}{{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}.
*{{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}} ({{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=extension|1=Enchō}}5 years) December 26 - <Completion of "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}"
*{{ill|lt=967|Q23699|qid=Q23699|1=967}} ({{ill|lt=Koyasu|Q1327182|qid=Q1327182|1=Kōhō}}4th year) July 9th - ``Engishiki'' implemented.
*1000 (Choho 2) - Minami-Shingu Shrine is founded (``Atsuta Daijingu Shintai Hessho'' <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Engi Yoshio Sequel 179">``Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Engi Yoshio Sequel (Part 1)'': page 179</ref>).**There is also a theory that it was founded in 1023 ({{ill|lt=Security (era name)|Q1150852|qid=Q1150852|lt=Security|1=Jian (era)}}3).
*1093 (Kanji 7) - ``I hereby decree that the Lords of Owari will rise from the shrine on the national fire'' (Hyakurensho <ref name="Kokushi Taikei 14_56">Kokushi Taikei Vol. 14: page 56</ref>).
**Indicates that a strange incident occurred at Hikami Aneko Shrine in which a fallen tree stood up, and a countermeasures meeting was held at the Imperial Court.
*1160 (Heiji 2) New Year - {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Q532301|qid=Q532301|1=Minamoto no Yoshitomo}}, who was defeated in the Heiji Rebellion, stops at Hikami Aneko Shrine and prays for the prosperity of the Genji clan and offers a sword ("Changshu Fushi" <ref name="Changshu Fushiichi 88">"Changshu Fushiichi": page 88</ref>).
*{{ill|lt=1190|Q19736|qid=Q19736|1=1190}} ({{ill|lt=Kenkyu|Q1193933|qid=Q1193933|1=Kenkyū}} first year) - {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|qid=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} dedicated the sword (jokurokujosha visit offering).
*{{ill|lt=1194|Q19743|qid=Q19743|1=1194}} (Kenkyu 5th year) and {{ill|lt=1195|Q19745|qid=Q19745|1=1195}} (Kenkyu 6th year) - Minamoto no Yoritomo dedicated the sword in two parts. Respectively, the envoy {{ill|lt=Hiromoto Oe|Q384829|qid=Q384829|1=Ōe no Hiromoto}} presented the envoy and returned to Tokyo.
*{{ill|lt=1335|Q6011|qid=Q6011|1=1335}} ({{ill|lt=Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|qid=Q1193970|lt=Kenmu|1=Kenmu}}2) - {{ill|lt=Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107|qid=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} dedicated a sword (visited on a pilgrimage to Kyoto).*{{ill|lt=1377|Q6289|qid=Q6289|1=1377}} ({{ill|lt=Eiwa (Japan)|Q1145780|qid=Q1145780|lt=Eiwa|1=Eiwa}}3 years) - Dedicated "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}" (Atsuta version).
*{{ill|lt=1382|Q6335|qid=Q6335|1=1382}} ({{ill|lt=Eitoku|Q1145805|qid=Q1145805|1=Eitoku}}3) - Fire at Hikami Aneko Shrine. As a result, the place name was changed from Hodaka Higami to Otaka Hikami.
*July 9, 1419 (June 17, 2016) - On the occasion of the transfer of the Watariden, {{ill|lt=Yoshimochi Ashikaga|Q466287|qid=Q466287|1=Ashikaga Yoshimochi}} dedicates a sword, a mirror, the Lotus Sutra, etc. (Atsuta Shrine Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu Volume 1": page 60</ref>).
*February 6, 1423 (December 25, 29th year of Oei) - Yoshimochi Ashikaga dedicates a sacred horse to the construction and transfer of Ohukuda Shrine, which was burnt down in 1415 (22nd year of Oei). Volume 1: pages 61-62</ref>).
*January 5, 1445 (November 27, 1st year of Bun'an) - The Ashikaga Shogun family dedicates a sword and a horse to the Gendayu Shrine Main Hall ("Atsuta Shrine Chronicles" <ref name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Volume 1": page 66</ref>).*July 9, 1451 (June 11, 1451) - The Ashikaga shogun family dedicated a sword and a horse to the construction of Omiya Toyoden ("Atsuta Shrine Chronicles" <ref name="Jingu Shrine Historical Materials Construction Sengu Volume 1": page 67</ref>).
*{{ill|lt=1560|Q6613|qid=Q6613|1=1560}} ({{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}3rd year) - {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}} dedicated {{ill|lt=Tsukiji wall|Q11603471|qid=Q11603471|1=tsuijibei}} (Nobunaga wall).
*{{ill|lt=1571|Q666|qid=Q666|1=Number of the beast}} ({{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|qid=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}}2nd year) - Oda Nobunaga repairs the shrine building and builds a new Kaizomon (Sea Gate)<ref name="atsutarekishi">[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/kankobunkakoryu/page/0000054494.html Historic sights that should be maintained and improved in Nagoya City 2]</ref>.
*March 31, 1572 (February 18, 3rd year of Genki) - {{ill|lt=Nobumori Sakuma|Q3275789|qid=Q3275789|1=Sakuma Nobumori}}, rebuilds Toyoden ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu 78">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Edition Volume 1": page 78</ref>).
*June 2, 1590 (May 1, Tensho 18) - {{ill|lt=Omandokoro|Q5353737|qid=Q5353737|1=Ōmandokoro}} visits the shrine and dedicates 123 kanmon as the firstfruits ("Atsuta Shrine Chronicles" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Zoei Sengu 71">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Zoei Sengu Edition Vol. 1": page 71</ref>).*1591 (Tensho 19) - {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} repairs the shrine building<ref name="atsutarekishi"/>.
*1593 (Bunroku 2) - Toyotomi Hideyoshi became the main owner, and the roof of the Seiden was repaired ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Zokei Sengu 8/78"/>).
*July 22, 1598 (June 19, 1598) - Emperor Toyotomi Hideyoshi (Atsutamiya Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 71"/>).
*1599 (Auspicious day of August 4th year of Keicho era) - {{ill|lt=Nagamasa Asano|Q1067546|qid=Q1067546|1=Asano Nagamasa}}, rebuilds the main hall of Hakkengu Shrine ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Volume 1": pages 78-79</ref>).
*1600 (Keicho 5) - {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} repairs the shrine, and {{ill|lt=Kato Kiyomasa|Q1069843|qid=Q1069843|1=Katō Kiyomasa}} constructs the Chinnomon Gate.<ref name="atsutarekishi"/>
*1601 (Auspicious day of June 6th year of Keicho era) - Hakkengu Shrine Roofing by Asano Nagamasa ("Atsuta Shrine Chronicles" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 72">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu Volume 1": page 72</ref>).
*1603 (July 8th year of Keicho) - Tokugawa Ieyasu restores Hakkengu Shrine ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Volume 1">"Atsuta Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Volume 1": page 79</ref>).*1606 (Auspicious day of September 11th year of Keicho era) - {{ill|lt=Hideyori Toyotomi|Q313641|qid=Q313641|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} orders {{ill|lt=Katsumoto Katagiri|Q1882775|qid=Q1882775|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} to rebuild the 9-ringed treasure pagoda of Jingu-ji Temple ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 179"/>).
*August 27, 1613 (July 12, Keicho 18) - {{ill|lt=Hidetada Tokugawa|Q314464|qid=Q314464|1=Tokugawa Hidetada}} (Atsutamiya Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 1st 72"/>).
*1618 (Auspicious day of March 4th year of Genna) - {{ill|lt=Hachisuka Kaemasa|Q1073557|qid=Q1073557|1=Hachisuka Iemasa}}, constructs Takaza Yumiko Shrine ("Gozoei Chronki" <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 79"/>).
*June 12, 1619 (May 1, 1619) - {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Yoshinao|Q1136186|qid=Q1136186|1=Tokugawa Yoshinao}}, the main building Tsukiji was repaired (Atsuta Shrine Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction and Sengu Volume 1">Page 73</ref>).
*January 29, 1623 (December 29, 1623) - Tokugawa Yoshinao repairs Kaizomon, Sanzo, Gakusho, Togaki, Shunshumon, and Gokusho (Atsutamiya Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 173"/>).
*December 21, 1629 (November 7, Kanei 6) - {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|qid=Q314481|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} dedicates 100 koku of rice as a first-fruits offering (Atsutamiya Chronicles <ref name="Jingu Historical Materials Construction Sengu 73"/>).*{{ill|lt=1839|Q7616|qid=Q7616|1=1839}} ({{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}10) January 19th - An attempt by a demon priest to steal the sacred object of Hakkengu Shrine.
*{{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} first year)
** March:〈{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance on the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}〉
** June: Announcement of Jingu name.
*{{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}} (Meiji 4) {{ill|lt=May 14th (lunar calendar)|Q839544|qid=Q839544|lt=May 14th|1=May 14 (Lunisolar)}} - With the establishment of {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rating system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was promoted to Kanpei Taisha.
*{{ill|lt=1893|Q7840|qid=Q7840|1=1893}} (Meiji 26) - Renovated from {{ill|lt=Owari-zukuri|Q11465322|qid=Q11465322|1=Owari-zukuri}} to Shinmei-zukuri.
*{{ill|lt=1914|Q2083|qid=Q2083|1=1914}} (Taisho 3) April 7: "Japanese Takeru 1800th Anniversary Festival" is held.
*August 1933 - The {{ill|lt=Nagoya Castle #Honmaru Palace|Q648629|qid=Q648629|lt=Nagoya Imperial Villa|1=Nagoya Castle}} Karikensho building, which was given to Atsuta Shrine by the Imperial Household Agency on the occasion of the Showa Tairei ceremony, is moved to Hikami Aneko Shrine and becomes a shrine of worship.
*{{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|qid=Q5240|1=1945}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}20)
**This year, the city suffered damage from several air raids.**May 17th - Various shrine buildings and the {{ill|lt=National Treasure|Q1139795|qid=Q1139795|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} Sea Gate <ref>[http://network2010.org/article/117 Network2010 (Nagoya/Atsuta Shrine Nobunaga Wall and Sea Gate in the Taisho period)]</ref> built by Oda Nobunaga were destroyed by fire.
**June 9 - Damaged by {{ill|lt=Atsuta air raid|Q11569122|qid=Q11569122|1=Bombing of Atsuta}}.
**July 29 - The national treasure Jinkomon Gate built by Kiyomasa Kato burns down.
**August 21st - The object of worship was moved to {{ill|lt=Hida Ichinomiya Mizunashi Shrine|Q704865|qid=Q704865|1=Minashi Shrine}}.
**September 19th - The object of worship was moved to Atsuta Shrine.
**December 15th - <{{ill|lt=Shinto Directive|Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}}>
*January 4, 1946 (Showa 21) - The main shrine of Kamichi Gama Shrine is rebuilt and the Kanza Festival is held.
*March 2, 1947 - The main shrine of Takaza Yumiko Shrine is rebuilt and the Kanza Festival is held.
*{{ill|lt=1955|Q18577|qid=Q18577|1=1955}} (Showa 30) October - Reconstruction.
*{{ill|lt=1963|Q2755|qid=Q2755|1=1963}} (1963) - Rebuilt Takaza Yumiko Shrine.*June 28, 1970 - Enshrining ceremony of Kotoseyama Shrine is held <ref name="Jingu 32">"Atsuta Jingu": page 32</ref>.
*{{ill|lt=2007|Q2024|qid=Q2024|1=2007}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}}19) October 22nd - Due to the renovation of the main shrine, the ``Kariden Transfer Ceremony'' will be held to move the deity to the temporary shrine.
*{{ill|lt=2009|Q1996|qid=Q1996|1=2009}} (Heisei 21) October 10th - The ``Honden Transfer Festival'' is held to move the deity to the main shrine.
*{{ill|lt=2013|Q1998|qid=Q1998|1=2013}} (Heisei 213) May 8th - "The 1900th Anniversary Festival" will be held.
*{{ill|lt=2021|Q49628|qid=Q49628|1=2021}} (Reiwa 4) - As part of the celebratory project commemorating the enthronement of the Emperor, Kusanagi Square (renovated) was opened on July 1st, and {{ill|lt=Treasure House of Swords Kusanagikan|Q110259947|qid=Q110259947|1=Treasure Trove of Swords Kusanagi-kan}} (newly built) was opened on October 3rd.

=== Visits by the Imperial Family in Recent Years ===
* {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}}November 11 (September 27, 1868): {{ill|lt=Emperor Meiji|Q174231|qid=Q174231|1=Emperor Meiji}}
* {{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|qid=Q7776|1=1878}} (Meiji 11): Emperor Meiji
* {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|qid=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39): Crown Prince Yoshihito (later {{ill|lt=Taisho Emperor|Q157052|qid=Q157052|1=Emperor Taishō}})
* {{ill|lt=1916|Q2087|qid=Q2087|1=1916}} (Taisho 5): Emperor Taisho
* {{ill|lt=1946|Q18610|qid=Q18610|1=1946}} (Showa 21): {{ill|lt=Emperor Showa|Q34479|qid=Q34479|1=Hirohito}}
* {{ill|lt=1957|Q5311|qid=Q5311|1=1957}} (Showa 32): Emperor Showa, {{ill|lt=Empress Kojun|Q230697|qid=Q230697|1=Empress Nagako}}<ref>{{Cite book |Japanese book |author=Imperial Household Agency |title=Twelfth True Story of Emperor Showa |publisher=Tokyo Shoseki |date=2017-03-28 |page=313 |isbn=978-4-487-74412-1}}</ref>.
* {{ill|lt=1991|Q2062|qid=Q2062|1=1991}} (1991): {{ill|lt=Emperor Akihito|UNKNOWN|lt=Emperor Akihito}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Empress Michiko|Q87610|qid=Q87610|lt=Empress Michiko|1=Empress Michiko}} (all at the time)
* {{ill|lt=2005|Q2019|qid=Q2019|1=2005}} (Heisei 17) July 12: Emperor Akihito and Empress Michiko (both were on the occasion of {{ill|lt=2005 Japan International Exposition|Q1054110|qid=Q1054110|1=Expo 2005}} visit)* {{ill|lt=2016|Q25245|qid=Q25245|1=2016}} (2016) August 8: Crown Prince Naruhito (currently {{ill|lt=current Emperor|Q8195432|qid=Q8195432|1=Reigning Emperor}})

=== Precincts ===

=== Kakiuchi ===
{{right|
<gallery widths="140px" heights="140px">
The placement of main shrine buildings in Atsuta Jingu Shrine, 2015.png|The placement of main shrine buildings in Atsuta Jingu Shrine, 2015.png|
Atsuta-jinguu shaden.JPG|View of Nakaju and Nakaju torii, Mizugaki Gomon, and main hall roof from the transept space<BR/> (July 2013)
Owari Famous Places Illustrated Guide. Part 1 Volume 3 Aichi District Atsuta Omiya Complete Map Part 2.jpg|{{ill|lt=Owari Zukuri|Q11465322|qid=Q11465322|1=Owari-zukuri}} ``Complete map of Atsuta Omiya, Part 2'' from Volume 3, Aichi District, Part 1)
</gallery>
}}
* Main shrine - The main shrine, which is the center of the main shrine, was originally built in {{ill|lt=1893|Q7840|qid=Q7840|1=1893}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}26), and has been renovated several times to date. The building area is 60.7 square meters, the architectural style is Shinmei-zukuri, the roof is covered with copper plates, and it is crowned with {{ill|lt=Chigi/Katsuogi|Q87220234|qid=Q87220234|lt=Chigi/Katsuogi|1=chigi and katsuogi}}. There are roundabouts and railings on all sides, and a wooden staircase on the south facade. The dugout pillars are set with stone.
* Tohoden
* Saihoden
* Nosha
* Mizugaki Kitamikado
* Mizugaki Gomon
* Guardhouse
* Tosai Gomon
* Saisai Gomon
* Inner Tamagaki Gate
* Transept
* Hall of worship
** Shihiroden
** Soto Tamagaki Gate

=== Others ===
* Doyouden
* Awarding place
* Kaguraden
* Saikan/Imperial Embassy
* Palace Office (Shrine Office)
* Atsuta Jingu Hall (wedding hall)
* Prayer Hall (car purification)
* Treasure
* Mikoshi warehouse
* Sairakusho
* Nobunaga Wall - {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}}3rd year ({{ill|lt=1560|Q6613|qid=Q6613|1=1560}}) {{ill|lt=May 19th (Lunar calendar)|Q839577|qid=Q839577|lt=May 19th|1=May 19 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and his troops stopped by on their way to {{ill|lt=Battle of Okehazama|Q349422|qid=Q349422|1=Battle of Okehazama}} and prayed for victory. After the battle, part of the wall that Nobunaga built as a thank you for his victory still exists, and is considered one of Japan's three greatest earthen walls.
* Large {{ill|lt=camphor tree|Q158722|qid=Q158722|lt=camphor tree|1=Camphora officinarum}}
* Senshukaku - A Sukiya-style tea room completed on June 25, {{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|qid=Q18597|1=1950}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}25). Renovated in July {{ill|lt=1989|Q2425|qid=Q2425|1=1989}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} first year).
* Guest hall* {{ill|lt=Matabee|Q122382294|qid=Q122382294|1=Matabei}} (National {{ill|lt=Registered Tangible Cultural Property|Q11579194|qid=Q11579194|1=Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan}}) - Former Sakagami family residence. {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} built in the middle of the {{ill|lt=Hida country|Q1056535|qid=Q1056535|lt=Hida|1=Hida Province}} region, {{ill|lt=1936|Q18649|qid=Q18649|1=1936}} (1937), moved to {{ill|lt=Showa Ward|Q1155273|qid=Q1155273|1=Shōwa-ku}}, {{ill|lt=1957|Q5311|qid=Q5311|1=1957}} (1957) {{ill|lt=Kinnosuke Jinno (2nd generation)|Q26485958|qid=Q26485958|lt=Kinnosuke Kamino|1=Kinnosuke Kamino}} and relocated it to its current location.
* Tea room "Seisetsuan" - A tea room located in the residence of Shinichi Ito, a tinsel merchant. {{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|qid=Q5165|1=1948}} It was moved to its current location in June 1948.
* Tea room "Rokuyuken" - Built by the Rokuyukai, a group of art dealers, and named by {{ill|lt=Takashi Masuda|Q786186|qid=Q786186|lt=Nuno Masuda|1=Masuda Takashi}}. In {{ill|lt=1947|Q5263|qid=Q5263|1=1947}} (Showa 22), it was dedicated by the Rokuyukai on the occasion of the establishment of the Atsuta Jingu Kencha Association, and was moved to its current location.
* Tea room "Hoan" - A Sukiya style tea room dedicated by Hikojiro Takahashi. {{ill|lt=1966|Q2649|qid=Q2649|1=1966}}Completed on October 1, 1966.
* Tea room "Homukentei" - Formerly a tea room. The name Hoken is derived from the haiku name Hoan, written by Hikojiro Takahashi, who renovated this tea ceremony room, and from Toyohiko Inui's ``inui.'' {{ill|lt=1977|Q2481|qid=Q2481|1=1977}} (1977) Restoration.* {{ill|lt=Ryukagekaku|Q11679252|qid=Q11679252|1=Ryōeikaku}} (National Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Former Nagoya Museum Exhibition Center. Built in {{ill|lt=1878|Q7776|qid=Q7776|1=1878}} (Meiji 11), moved to Shonai Park in {{ill|lt=1932|Q18743|qid=Q18743|1=1932}} (Showa 7), and moved to its current location in {{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|qid=Q2644|1=1968}} (Showa 43).
* Minamo Temple
* Garden "Magatama-en" - A pond with a curved ball pond {{ill|lt=circular garden|Q11420231|qid=Q11420231|1=Strolling garden}}.
* {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine Treasure Hall|Q11569116|qid=Q11569116|lt=Treasure Hall|1=Atsuta Jingū Museum}} (Culture Hall) - It has approximately 6,000 items donated by {{ill|lt=National Treasure|Q1139795|qid=Q1139795|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}, {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}, {{ill|lt=Imperial Family|Q909452|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tokugawa Shogun Family|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Owari Tokugawa Family|Q841740|qid=Q841740|1=Owari Tokugawa family}}, etc.
* Treasure Hall Annex
* Sakuma Toro - When the fourth son {{ill|lt=Katsuyuki Sakuma|Q10885889|qid=Q10885889|lt=Katsuyuki|1=Sakuma Katsuyuki}} of Gokidokoro Castle Lord {{ill|lt=Moritsugu Sakuma|Q10885892|qid=Q10885892|1=Sakuma Moritsugu}} encountered a typhoon at sea, he visited Atsuta Shrine. When he prayed to the shrine for protection, he escaped the disaster, so he donated it as a thank you in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}}7 ({{ill|lt=1630|Q6856|qid=Q6856|1=1630}}). <ref>From the local information board</ref> Known as one of Japan's three major lanterns.
* Nijugocho Bridge
* Minamiike Pond
* Kusanagi Square
* {{ill|lt=Treasure House of Swords Kusanagikan|Q110259947|qid=Q110259947|1=Treasure Trove of Swords Kusanagi-kan}}
* Seisetsu-mon (Unopened Gate) - Former main shrine north gate.
* Reiwa Memorial Hall* Dohyo
* Aichi Prefectural Shrine Agency
* Atsuta Jingu Academy

=== Betsugu/Setsusha ===
{{Anchors|Bekku|Setsusha}}Within the main shrine, there are 1 annex shrine, 8 setsushas, and 19 subsidiary shrines within the precincts, and 4 setshas and 12 subsidiary shrines outside the precincts, making a total of 45 shrines (including the main shrine). |group="Note"}}<ref>[http://www.atsutajingu.or.jp/jingu/about/yukari/ Bekku, Setsusha, Suesha] (Atsuta Jingu official website). </ref>.

===Buildings of the past===
There used to be three gates connected to the Nobunaga wall, the Kaizomon (Kaizomon), which was designated as a national treasure in {{ill|lt=1920|Q2155|qid=Q2155|1=1920}} (Taisho 9), and the Chinkomon and Shunshomon, which are also national treasures, but they were destroyed by fire during the Nagoya Air Raid during the Pacific War. After the war, the cultural properties of Atsuta Shrine that had been lost in the war were not rebuilt or restored. In {{ill|lt=2022|Q49625|qid=Q49625|1=2022}} (Reiwa 4), Atsuta City's history experience project <ref>[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/atsuta/page/0000155998.html Atsuta City's history experience project "Atsuta History Exploration" has begun! (PDF: 6306KB) - As part of the {{ill|lt=Augmented Reality|Q254183|qid=Q254183|1=Augmented reality}} project as part of Nagoya City]</ref>, the Sea Gate was commissioned and supervised by architect {{ill|lt=Masayuki Miura|Q11356475|qid=Q11356475|1=Masayuki Miura}}{{ill|lt=Hiroshima University|Q115220|qid=Q115220|1=Hiroshima University}}. A reconstruction map of the Maritime Gate was created based on materials such as traces of the foundation stone of the Upper Gate, and the Maritime Gate was reproduced with 3DCG <ref>[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/atsuta/cmsfiles/contents/0000155/155998/panelAR.pdf Restoration of Atsuta Jingu Maritime Gate, a former national treasure (PDF: 6306KB) - Nagoya City]</ref>.

{{right|
<gallery widths="140px" heights="140px" perrow="2">Postcard of Gate Chinko-Mon Atsuta Shrine Scan10005.jpg|Picture postcard of Chinko-Mon Atsuta Shrine (no longer extant), a former national treasure building
Postcard of Gate Kaijo-Mon Atsuta Shrine Scan10007.jpg|Picture postcard of the former national treasure building, Maritime Gate (Kaizomon, no longer extant)
</gallery>
}}
* Kaizomon (Kaizomon)
* Shunshumon
* Chinhuangmon
* Gonden
* Nagaden
* Ritual vessel storage
* Old government office
* Former Palace Office
* Old funeral hall
* Former Imperial Embassy
* {{ill|lt=Atsuta Shrine Noh Theater|Q11569117|qid=Q11569117|1=Nōgakuden, Atsuta Jingū}} (closed in 2006)

* {{ill|lt=Emperor's Birthday|Q72318|qid=Q72318|lt=Tencho Festival|1=The Emperor's Birthday}} ({{ill|lt=February 23rd|Q2352|qid=Q2352|1=February 23}})

=== Cultural assets ===
Because the Kusanagi sword is a sacred object, many swords have been donated to Atsuta Shrine from the local community, and the shrine has approximately 450 swords in its collection. More than 30 of them are designated cultural properties by the country and prefecture. October 4, 2021</ref>.
[[File:Hokekyo Yujutsubon, Atsuta Jingu Shrine.jpg|thumb|180px|Paperback, colored Lotus Sutra, exhibited]]
[[File:Mirror box with Mount Penglai, Atsuta Jingu, Aichi, Japan, Muromachi period, 1400s AD, maki-e lacquer - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC08862.jpg|thumb|180px|Hōrai Makie Mirror Box Lid]][[File:Mirror box with Mount Penglai, Atsuta Jingu, Aichi, Japan, Muromachi period, 1400s AD, maki-e lacquer - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC08870.jpg|thumb|180px|Hōrai makie mirror box lid and body]]
[[File:Mirror, from the Mirror box with Mount Penglai, Atsuta Jingu, Aichi, Japan, Muromachi period, 1400s AD - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC08867.jpg|thumb|180px|Eight-edge mirror with pine and bamboo crane design]]

=== National Treasure ===
*Tanto Meira Kuni Toshi November 1997

=== Important cultural property ===
* Paper version of the Lotus Sutra in color
* 12 wooden bugaku masks (1 Ryōō, 2 Nosori, 1 Kanjōraku, 4 Kunlun Hachisen, 2 Ninomai, 1 Nukitou, 1 Takitoku)
* Chrysanthemum makie hand basket
* Mirror box
** Shochiku twin crane pattern circular mirror, paulownia phoenix makie mirror box
** Shochiku twin crane pattern eight-edge mirror, Hourai maki-e mirror box
** Shochiku twin crane pattern eight-edge mirror, Hourai maki-e mirror box
* All ancient sacred treasures (details will be given later)
* A gilt-bronze saddle set (with 3 black lacquered saddles and accessories, 1 volume of decorative saddle drawings) (Details will be given later) <ref group="Note">Some of the accessories were additionally designated in 2015 (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 144 of September 4, 2015). </ref>
* Gold bronze Hyogo chain sword
* Tachi Mei Kunitomo
* Tachi Meinorikuni
* Sword Meiyoshimitsu
* Tachi, March 3rd year of the Meiryokai Kagen era, Yamashiro province resident Kuro-sa (hereinafter referred to as kiri)
* Wakizashi signature Kuninobu Hasebe
* Short sword inscribed by Kuninobu Hasebe (cut with inscription) Tomoyoshi Fujiwara
* Tanto Mei Kunimitsu Gentoku 3rd year (hereafter cut)
* Tachi, famous by Sokichisaku (designated as an important cultural property in 1911)
* Tachi, famous by Sokichisaku (designated as an important cultural property in 1912)
* Sword Name Tamekiyo with Atsuta Taijingu Munehisa and Kiritsuke on the body
* Tachi Mei Nagamitsu
* Long sword signed Bishū Osafune Kanemitsu
* Long sword signed Bishu Osafune Shigemitsu
* Tachi inscription: June 1st, 1995, Jitsuasaku, with an inscription donated by Bunji 4th year, Shukatsu, on the ground.
* Tsurugi mei□li (holi)
* Tachi Inscription: Masayuki. On the front is Yokuro Okubo, 8th day of the 8th month of the 2nd year of Shinmi, and on the back is a cut with ``Atsuta Daimyoshin Hodono Shinyuki.''
* Tachi Mumei (Denshincho)
* Wakizashi On the front of the inscription is a dedication to Bishu Atsuta Daimyojin, and on the back of the finger is the words Yasutsugu Echizen, who was summoned to the two Imperial Palaces, and the Gomon of the Bushu Edo Imperial Guards.* Nihon Shoki (paper-backed waka kaishi), 15 volumes, 2nd and 3rd year of Eiwa 1st year, Joa donation letter (attached to 1 volume of donation letter on Shimotsuki 4th year of Eiwa 3)
* News of Emperor Gohanazono Shinkan December 12, Eikyo 5 (attached to Ashikaga Yoshinori's internal notes)

Authority: For properties designated up to 2000, refer to ``National Treasures and Important Cultural Properties Encyclopedia, Special Volume'' (Comprehensive Catalog by Owner, Name Index, Statistical Materials) (Mainichi Shimbun, 2000) (The ``G'' has been changed to the modern notation).

There were also the Maritime Gate and the Chinhuang Gate, both former national treasures, but they were destroyed by air raids during World War II.

 <div class="NavContent" style="text-align:left;">
'''Ancient Treasures'''
*Outerwear (Uwagi) Moe yellow small hollyhock ground paulownia bamboo phoenix pattern double weave 2nd area

The original designated date was February 19, 1957. In 1977, 1 cypress fan, 5 dressing tables, 1 crown box, 1 ryo, 3 vermilion lacquer bows, and 1 flat koshi were additionally designated (Ministry of Education Notification No. 116, June 11, 1977). In 2014, 2 red lacquer bows and 10 azusa bows were additionally designated (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 117 of August 21, 2014).

*1 pair of obstacle mud

On June 6, 1987, it was designated as an important cultural property as ``Gold-bronze-covered saddle with one tool, 2 black lacquered saddles, 1 volume of decorative saddle drawings''. The following properties were additionally designated as of September 4, 2015 (Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 144 of September 4, 2015).
:Among the accessories of ``Golden-Bronze Karakura'', 1 each of ``Men-tsunagi'', ``Chest-tsunagi'', and ``Shiri-tsunagi'', as well as ``Kai'', ``Shodei'', ``Saddle bed'', ``Kokei'', and ``Sawa'' are included. One of the three designated black lacquer saddles. All of the designated properties from ``Men Tsunagi'' to ``Rikihaku Zanchi''.

=== National Registered Tangible Cultural Property ===
* Matabei (former Sakagami family residence)
* Ryukagekaku (formerly Nagoya Museum Exhibition Center)

=== Aichi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property ===
* 11 colored Atsuta Shrine old illustrations on paper
* Shinto ritual mask (Rooji) page 1 - Nanbokucho period.
* Shinto ritual mask (middle-aged man) page 1 - Nanbokucho period.
* Shinto ritual mask (young person) page 1 - Muromachi period.
* Shinto ritual mask (young person) 1 - Early Muromachi period.
* Tachi Tomoshige (Echizen Province) 1 piece - Late Kamakura period (Nanbokucho period).
* Tachi Naotane (Musashi Province) 1 mouth - Kaei 2 (1849).
* Tachi Mori (Bichu) 1 mouth - Kamakura period.
* Tachi Yukihira (Bungo Province) 1 piece - Kamakura period.
* One side of the eight-ridge mirror with azuika and double phoenix pattern - late Heian period, Engishiki style Japanese mirror.
* Mirror with bird, beast, and flower patterns, page 1 - Late Tang or Heian period, pattern in the Chinese style.
* One side of the eight-sided mirror with azuika-souran pattern - late Heian period.
* Five-flower mirror with azure flower arabesque and twin birds pattern - 1 page - From the late Heian period to the early Kamakura period.
*Ssangyong cloud pattern mirror 1 side - Yuan Dynasty, large bronze mirror.
*Shakuhachi 1 pipe - Early Muromachi period, 1 section, 5 holes.
* Large bell (signed in the first year of Tenbun) 1 mouth - 1st year of Tenbun (1532).
* Wakizashi Nagato no kami Fujiwara Ujigumo 1 mouth - Keicho 13 (1608).
* Tachi inscription Kanetake 1 mouth - Keicho 16 (1611).
*Shunshomon plaque page 1 - Kamakura period, (traditionally) written by Ono Michifu.
* 1 guzheng - Muromachi period, ceremonial use, aristocratic style.
* 1 set of Tetsu (inscribed in the 5th year of Kenmu) - Inscribed in the 5th year of Kenmu (1338).
* Tanto inscribed Kuninobu Hasebe 1 piece - Bunwa era.
* Short sword inscribed Muramasa 1 piece - Muromachi period.
* Tachi inscribed Kanefusa 1 piece - Eiroku 11 (1568).
* Kurikara sword 1 piece - Muromachi period.* Two large-sized commemorative coins for Emperor Meiji - 1860.
* 1 cast iron fishing lantern - Shoho 4 (1647).
* 1 gilt bronze nail cover - Eisho 14 (1517).
* 1 drum - used for Shinto rituals in the Muromachi period.
* 1 taiko drum - Used for Shinto rituals during the Heian period.
* Aogai pear-shaped saddle with hollow pattern 1st back - Nanbokucho period.
* 5 rice bowls included: 1 porridge ladle and 1 rice bowl - Muromachi period, for ritual use.
* Wakizashi Mumei 1 mouth - From the Heian period to the Kamakura period, the name Azamaru.
* Wakizashi name Omote Yoshimitsu Ura Kame Omaru 1 mouth - Legend has it as Kumo Kiri Maru during the Kamakura period.
* Atsuta Shrine Stepping Song Festival Ode Volume 2 - From the Kamakura period to the Muromachi period, rare odes from the Bun'ei era, 1. Bun'ei 7th year, 2. Tenbun 15th year.
* Atsuta Shrine repair proposal 2 volumes, 1 width - Muromachi period, repair materials, 1. March date of Bunmei 11, 2. Tenbun 11, 3. Bishu Atsuta Shrine main line and order remaining letters.
* Heart Sutra in gold letters on dark blue paper, Volume 1 - Kamakura period, sermon hearing map on the endpaper.
* Extra-fine Lotus Sutra Volume 2 - Tokuji 3rd year, copies of the Lotus Sutra and materials for shrine worship.
*``Atsuta Taijingu'' divine name 1 width - Edo period, probably written by Emperor Gomizunoo.
* Lotus Sutra Comfortable Gourmet Volume 1 - Kamakura period, scroll book, decorative cover.
* Amida Sutra Volume 1 - Kamakura period, decorative paper ink calligraphy.
* Heart Sutra Volume 1 - 3rd year of the Karei era, ink and paper.
* Letter from Sanmihoin Jozeki (Yoshifusa Miyoshi) 1 page - Edo period, ink and paper on paper.
* Horaku Kasen Renga Kaishi, 1 volume - Muromachi period, 1996, sketch on writing paper.* 312 chapters of Atsuta Manku of 13th and 4th Kan'ei years - Edo period, historical material on haiku literature, 1. Atsuta Manku of 13th Kanei period, 2. Added Atsuta Manku of 13th Kanei period, 3. Manku of Atsuta of 14th year of Kanei era (Class A), 4. Manku of Atsuta of 14th year of Kanei era (Class B).
* Atsuta Shrine Baba Family Documents Volume 8 - Jingu worship materials from the Kamakura period to the Edo period.
* 1 piece of iron gilt-copper-clad horse harness - Kofun period, dedicated in 1993, excavated from Shiratori Tumulus, archaeological material.

=== Notes ===
* In {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} established in the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, Ichinomiya in Owari Province was {{ill|lt=Masuita Shrine|Q705916|qid=Q705916|1=Masumida Shrine}} and Atsuta Shrine was considered Sannomiya. There are various theories regarding this, and {{ill|lt=Kanji Inoue|Q11372970|qid=Q11372970|1=Hiroshi Inoue}} states that Atsuta Shrine was initially treated as a special shrine.{{Efn2|In the June 1st year of Eiman era, the Order of the Shrines of the Jingikan (Heian Ibun No. 3358), it was written as ``Owari Province Ichinomiya Ninomiya Atsuta Shrine,'' and Atsuta Shrine was not considered to be a Sannomiya Shrine. }} and the active efforts of Masumita Shrine <ref>Kanji Inoue, "Japan's Medieval State and the Ichinomiya System of the Provinces," Iwata Shoin, 2009, pp. 95–98. </ref>The theory is that {{ill|lt=Toru Ueshima|Q24576799|qid=Q24576799|1=Susumu Uejima}} initially had the status of Ichinomiya, but later the conflict between the Kokuga and Atsuta Shrine influenced the change of Ichinomiya. </ref> is issued. In any case, at the end of the Heian period (late 12th century), when the order of domestic shrines centered on Ichinomiya was established, Atsuta Jingu came to be positioned as Sannomiya, and Atsuta Jingu avoided calling itself Sannomiya by using expressions such as ``Chinju Sansha'' <ref>Owari Kokushicho Sendan (Kamakura Ibun No. 3401) dated 1st year of Karoku</ref>.*{{ill|lt=Saigyou|Q224689|qid=Q224689|1=Saigyō}} "Nijyugocho Bridge", where legend has it that a monk sat down to rest.{{Efn2|It consists of 25 stone slabs. }} appears in {{ill|lt=Owari Meisho Zue|Q11465290|qid=Q11465290|1=Owari meisho zue}} and Nagoya Jinku, and is said to be the oldest stone bridge in Nagoya.
* In the {{ill|lt=All Japan Collegiate Ekiden Championships|Q11390082|qid=Q11390082|1=Japan Collegiate Ekiden Championships}} to be held in November, Nishimon Torii-mae ({{ill|lt=National Route 19|Q899326|qid=Q899326|1=Japan National Route 19}}) will be used as the starting point.
* At the {{ill|lt=1936 Berlin Olympics|Q8150|qid=Q8150|lt=Berlin Olympics|1=1936 Summer Olympics}} in {{ill|lt=1936|Q18649|qid=Q18649|1=1936}}, female swimmer {{ill|lt=Hideko Maehata|Q271612|qid=Q271612|1=Hideko Maehata}} swallowed several Atsuta Shrine charms and placed them in her stomach just before the race, winning the gold medal.
* Ryukagekaku, built as a resting place for {{ill|lt=Emperor Meiji|Q174231|qid=Q174231|1=Emperor Meiji}}, is registered as a national {{ill|lt=Registered Tangible Cultural Property|Q11579194|qid=Q11579194|1=Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan}}. From the north window, you can see the Nobunaga Wall, one of Japan's three major earthen walls, dedicated to {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, and from the south window, you can see Magatama Pond and the garden, which is colored with seasonal flowers.
* The pond inside Magatama-en that is modeled after {{ill|lt=Magatama|Q334232|qid=Q334232|1=Magatama}}, one of the {{ill|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|Q327465|qid=Q327465|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is called Magatama (Magatama) Pond. One of the bridges that spans there is a bridge called ``Akahashi'', and it is said that two people who swear their love on this bridge will be happy, so the locals call it ``Fukuyosebashi'', a bridge that brings good fortune.

=== Atsuta Okage Yokocho Plan ===Atsuta Shrine used to have a thriving temple town, so there are plans to recreate a bustling temple town like Ise's {{ill|lt=Okage Yokocho|Q11261371|qid=Q11261371|1=Okage Yokochō}} near Atsuta Shrine.{{ill|lt=2011 The concept was conceived in|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}}<ref>[http://www.nup.or.jp/nui/user/media/document/investigation/h22/shimin1.pdf Atsuta Shrine's gate street. ．． ．． - Nagoya Town Development Corporation top page]</ref>. The maintenance plan itself was {{ill|lt=Jingumae Station|Q6202481|qid=Q6202481|lt=Meitetsu Jingumae Station|1=Jingū-mae Station}} of Atsuta Jingu Shrine, which Nagoya City called "Atsuta Sando City" in {{ill|lt=1988|Q2426|qid=Q2426|1=1988}}. There was a redevelopment plan to improve the area including the shopping street, but due to various circumstances the plan failed and was set back <ref>[https://milepost.exblog.jp/28548730/ What was the redevelopment plan for the Atsuta Jingu Station area 30 years ago?: Record of town development activities (Nagoya Gakuin University Mizuno)]</ref>. {{ill|lt=Takashi Kawamura|Q440000|qid=Q440000|1=Takashi Kawamura (politician)}} Former Nagoya mayor also pledged to improve ``Atsuta Kusanagi Yokocho'' during the April mayoral election. {{ill|lt=2018|Q25291|qid=Q25291|1=2018}}Atsuta-Jingu Station Area Town Development Council was launched in April <ref>[https://www.city.nagoya.jp/jutakutoshi/cmsfiles/contents/0000057/57993/atsutakousou.pdf Atsuta-Jingu Station Area Town Development Plan]</ref>. {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|qid=Q49619|1=2024}} In July, Nagoya City once again presented a redevelopment plan for Jingumae Station. Nihon Keizai Shimbun]</ref>. On {{ill|lt=September 6th|Q2858|qid=Q2858|1=September 6}} in 2024, {{ill|lt=Atsuta nagAya|Q130240709|qid=Q130240709|1=Atsuta nagAya}} by {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railway|Q30850|qid=Q30850|1=Meitetsu}}, which is different from the Nagoya City plan mentioned above, began operations west of Meitetsu Jingumae Station.

=== Tradition ===
*The Atsuta area of Atsuta Shrine has been known as ``{{ill|lt=Horai|Q798391|qid=Q798391|lt=Horaijima|1=Mount Penglai}}'' since ancient times, and was even written about in books from the Kamakura period. Since ancient times, {{pronounced kana|Noshio Ichigata|Ayuchigata}}{{Efn2|{{pronounced kana|Nengoichigata|Ayuchigata}} is {{jalink|Aichi County} which appeared at the end of the {{ill|lt=Ice Age|Q49367|qid=Q49367|lt=Ice Age|1=Ice age}} due to a sea level rise called {{ill|lt=Jomon Transgression|Q834405|qid=Q834405|1=Holocene glacial retreat}}. (Aichi Prefecture)|Q552687|Formerly Aichi District}} refers to the {{ill|lt=tidal flat|Q31796|qid=Q31796|1=Mudflat}} that spread throughout the area. }}, Atsuta Shrine, which is overgrown with old pine cedar trees over 1,000 years old, looks like a cape jutting out into the sea, and was likened to being on the shell of a giant turtle.Therefore, Atsuta Shrine was referred to as Horai Island (in books introducing Japan), the home of the immortal {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shins|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is said that this is because it was imitated by a Chinese legend from the Tang Dynasty. Atsuta ``The Legend of Hourai'']</ref>.*Atsuta Shrine has a legend related to {{ill|lt=Yang Guifei|Q298046|qid=Q298046|1=Yang Guifei}}. During the {{ill|lt=Tang|Q9683|qid=Q9683|1=Tang dynasty}} period in China, the ambitious {{ill|lt=Xuanzong (Tang)|Q9746|qid=Q9746|lt=Emperor Xuanzong|1=Emperor Xuanzong of Tang}} was waiting for an opportunity to invade Japan. The Japanese gods who knew about this quickly got together and discussed how to deal with it, and as a result, Atsuta Daimyojin transformed into an assassin and an unparalleled beauty, approached Genso, and used her beauty to seduce Genso so that he would not invade Japan. Although his first mission was successful, his true identity was revealed and he was later killed. The spirit of Atsuta Daimyojin, which had transformed into Yang Guifei, returned to Atsuta. Based on the legend of Yang Guifei, a tomb for Yang Guifei was built at Shimizu Shrine, but it was demolished during the renovation of Atsuta Shrine in the early Meiji period. {{Citation required|date=August 2025}}

* {{ill|lt=Ko Atsuta|Q11569110|qid=Q11569110|1=Isao Atsuta}} - Inherited the surname Atsuta as a historian and son-in-law.
* {{ill|lt=Omiya Atsuta Shrine|Q11434437|qid=Q11434437|1=Omiya-atsuta Shrine}} ({{ill|lt=Nagano Prefecture|Q127877|qid=Q127877|1=Nagano Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Matsumoto City|Q213324|qid=Q213324|1=Matsumoto, Nagano}}) - A shrine where Atsuta Okami was enshrined from Atsuta Shrine.
* {{ill|lt=Omiya Shrine (Iwade City)|Q11434451|qid=Q11434451|1=Omiya Shrine (Iwade City)}} ({{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|qid=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwade City|Q579623|qid=Q579623|1=Iwade, Wakayama}}) - It is said that during the reign of Emperor Genmei (712), Nihon Takeru was solicited from Atsuta Shrine in Owari and it became a village shrine.* {{ill|lt=Owarido Shrine|Q11465305|qid=Q11465305|1=Owari-to Shrine}} (Aichi Prefecture Nagoya City {{ill|lt=Moriyama Ward|Q1155291|qid=Q1155291|1=Moriyama-ku, Nagoya}}, {{ill|lt=Seto City|Q499493|qid=Q499493|1=Seto, Aichi}}) - This shrine is said to have been built in 5th year of the 13th Emperor, {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|qid=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}}, at the request of Miyazakihime, to enshrine the founder of the Owari clan.It is a shrine located on the summit of {{ill|lt=Higashiyayama|Q11528351|qid=Q11528351|1=Mount Togoku}}, the sacred mountain of the Owari clan, and is also known as ``Atsuta no Okunoin.''
* {{ill|lt=Judango|Q11404985|qid=Q11404985|lt=Fujidango|1=Fujidango}} (Judango) - A famous sweet sold around Atsuta Shrine.
* {{ill|lt=Kaminoi Sake Brewery|Q22125473|qid=Q22125473|1=Kaminoi Shuzo}} - A sake brewing company named after the third generation head of the family donated the Saita of Atsuta Shrine.
* {{ill|lt=Kitaka Chiaki|Q48760362|qid=Q48760362|1=Senshū Suetaka}} - A member of the House of Peers' Baron from the Daiguji Chiaki clan.
* {{ill|lt=Atsuta Jingu Denmacho Station|Q970033|qid=Q970033|1=Atsuta Jingu Temma-cho Station}} (nearest station to the south gate)
* {{ill|lt=Atsuta Jingu Nishi Station|Q903638|qid=Q903638|1=Atsuta Jingu Nishi Station}} (nearest station)
* {{ill|lt=Atsuta nagAya|Q130240709|qid=Q130240709|1=Atsuta nagAya}}
